They Judged by Quality, not Quantity. When you thought the franchise concluded in 2010, a 4th instalment was decided, not even Pixar aninators asked for this. I did not get into it as much as others. Maybe, I would care because it is "Toy Story" & a bias on nostalgia. You did not really need to have some big stars like Keanu Reaves portraying a character (Duke Kaboom) & is it funny how the toy rabbit & duck at the carnival were voiced by men. I believe that it was a miracle to reintroduce Bo Peep, who seems more of a girlboss & she was absent in the predecessor. At least we got some funny moments from characters like Duke Kaboom. I could never believe that Gabby Gabby is actually villainous - she was not chosen before & what was her voice box? At least she had somebody to love. Loved the family Andersons dilemmas. Isn't the flashback scene at the start inconsistent? Can't wait for what happens in "Toy Story 5"?, although the franchise got apparently tiresome?
I knew i was going to enjoy it although I was nowhere near prepared for how much emotion it was going to bring.

Hey man, this movie’s overhated. While it’s not on the same level as the first three, I found this to be an undeniably poignant, emotionally fulfilling epilogue to the trilogy. Because that’s what it is: an epilogue. Sure, it’s called Toy Story 4, but if you interpret it through that lens, this is really about Woody’s story coming to a close, and symbolically, what life is like as a parent after your child has left you. And so I found Woody’s decision to not only be in-character at this point in time, but also to make sense thematically. It attains the franchise’s overarching narrative about growing up, and letting go, and what change looks like as you move forward in your life. It’s a bit more somber, and not as thrilling as an adventure because of this. It’s the most flawed of the four films, and the absence of most of the other iconic characters definitely hurts it. But overall, I found this to be a poignant, satisfying send-off for Woody, and at the time, this franchise.

A Safe, Heartwarming Adventure for Kids I am very picky about what my kids watch, and Toy Story 4 exceeded my expectations. What I loved most is that the movie didn’t include any mean or destructive characters, which made it a safe and positive experience for young viewers. At the same time, it’s exciting, funny, and heartwarming, with beautiful animation and a strong story. It’s a movie my kids could enjoy without any worry, and it even kept me entertained as a parent. Highly recommend for families looking for an engaging, wholesome film.

★★★★★ Toy Story 4 is the miracle nobody expected: a fourth chapter that had zero business existing, yet somehow delivers one of the most beautiful, bold, and emotionally mature endings any franchise has ever dared to give its hero. This time it’s not about being played with or being left behind; it’s about finding the courage to choose your own purpose when the old one no longer fits. Woody’s journey from loyal sheriff to free-spirited lost toy is handled with such tenderness and honesty that it feels less like a kids’ movie and more like Pixar gently guiding an entire generation into adulthood. The final five minutes (that quiet, perfect goodbye on the carousel while "You’ve Got a Friend in Me" plays one last time) is one of the most soul-shattering moments in animation history. I’m not crying, you’re crying. The new characters are instant classics: Keegan-Michael Key and Jordan Peele as Ducky & Bunny are comedy gold, Tony Hale’s Forky is the most hilariously existential spork ever created ("I am not a toy… I AM TRASH!"), Christina Hendricks’ Gabby Gabby delivers the best villain-redemption arc in the series, and Keanu Reeves’ Duke Caboom is Canada’s greatest contribution to cinema since maple syrup. Visually it’s the most stunning Toy Story yet (those rain-drenched antique-store shots and the carnival at night are pure eye-candy), the action sequences (the RV chase, the store finale) are thrilling, and Randy Newman somehow still had one perfect new song left in him. Toy Story 4 doesn’t just add another adventure; it completes Woody’s character arc in a way the previous three films never could have alone. It’s brave, it’s funny, it’s heartbreaking, and it leaves you walking out of the theater feeling weirdly… okay with growing up. A perfect, unexpected gift. 10/10. To infinity… and your own path. Thank you for everything, Woody. We’ll miss you, partner. 🪁❤️🚐

I struggle to see why this was needed after 3 just closed everything perfectly. And after seeing this....yeah I still struggle with why this exists. It's got really good animation. Quality wise it's the best of the franchise. Solid soundtrack but it sounds like the others and doesn't really stand out on its own. Everything else is good about it but is a signiifcant setep down storytelling wise. It's basically a road movie adventure which is a cool idea. But the new characters are a mixed bag. The two comedic relief toys are great. Forky is....something...he's alright but my god they could've dialed back the idiot in him. His whole character is just being an idiot. Bo Beep is good but my god it feels like she dominates the runtime and overshadows everyone else. The underlying message feels muddled also. So toys don't need kids but the villian finds purpose by doing the most noble thing a toy can do...which is be with a kid? It feels like also we kind of just forget about Bonnie as well. Another issue is it feels like it explores issues that were done before in the previous trilogy and not as clearly. Than it kind of just forgets about all the legacy characters and feel ike an afterthought. It bounces between Woody and the gang for most of the middle and it feels like two seperate films. But it does bring it together with a nice ending that is cool to see. Again though I struggle to see why this was needed but anyone who is a fan of the franchise, the director, any voice actors in this, road movies, or animated adventures should check this out. I'm worried if they keep making these the quality will just keep falling.
